attributes.stem: A 62-year-old man presents with two month history of left shoulder pain, as well as intermittent pain and numbness in the thumb and index finger of his left hand. The pain appears to radiate to the outer aspect of his forearm. His symptoms are starting to interfere with his job as an electrician. His past medical history is significant for diabetes (last HbA1c 7.5%) and osteoarthritis of the hips and knees. On examination there is reduced tactile sensation over his L thumb and index finger, with no obvious thenar eminence wasting. Elbow flexion and wrist extension on this side is reduced, with power graded 3/5. The left brachioradialis reflex is absent. Examination of the cervical spine reveals no focal tenderness, although Spurling test is positive. The rest of the physical examination, including examination of the right side, is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The correct answer to this question is cervical radiculopathy.

Cervical radiculopathy is a condition where there is compression and/or irritation of a nerve root as it exits the cervical spine. The most common nerve roots involved are C6 and C7. Spondylosis (vertebral column degeneration) causes 70% of cases of cervical radiculopathy, with stenosis of vertebral foramina being the usual underlying mechanism for nerve root entrapment.

This patient's age and history of osteoarthritis are risk factors for cervical radiculopathy. His symptoms are consistent with this diagnosis, with pain and numbness confined to the C6 dermatome, as well as shoulder pain, which is a common finding. Examination further supports a diagnosis of cervical radiculopathy, with a number of C6 nerve root deficits found including:

  • reduced tactile sensation of index finger and thumb

  • reduced power of elbow flexion and wrist extension

  • absent brachioradialis reflex

The Spurling test (where the patient is asked to extend and laterally rotate their neck, whilst an axial force is being applied) has a high specificity for cervical radiculopathy of up to 94%, but a relatively low sensitivity of around 40-65%. Given the high specificity, the presence of this sign makes cervical radiculopathy a likely diagnosis.

Idiopathic brachial neuritis (also known as Parsonage-Turner syndrome) is a good differential and can often be confused with cervical radiculopathy, however there are a few differentiating features. Thought to be related to an autoimmune-mediated process, the condition can be triggered by upper respiratory tract infections, immunisation, smoking and emotional stress. Its onset is usually heralded by severe pain at sites such as the shoulder region, trapezius ridge, scapular area, upper arm, forearm, or hand. This pain can last from hours to weeks. This pain is then followed by weakness, which tends to develop over a period of days. The weakness can take up to 18 months to resolve. Whilst sensory deficits can be observed, most patients will have weakness as their primary complaint. This patient is more concerned with his sensory symptoms. Furthermore, idiopathic brachial neuritis affects multiple nerve roots of the brachial plexus, so the pattern of muscle weakness will be reflective of this. As such, idiopathic brachial neuritis is not the correct answer.

Diabetic neuropathy is not the correct answer. Symptoms of diabetic neuropathy are often bilateral and will affect the lower limbs before the upper limbs, more often than not. Furthermore, this patient has reasonable control of his diabetes, with an HbA1c of 7.5%.

Thoracic outlet syndrome is not the correct answer. This condition occurs when there is compression of neuro-vascular structures at the level of the thoracic outlet. Patients can present with a variety of symptoms related to compression of either nerves, arteries or veins, or sometimes all 3. In this case, the localised nature of the symptoms and signs combined with the lack of any obvious aetiology that might explain a new-onset of thoracic outlet syndrome (e.g. cervical trauma), makes this diagnosis less likely than cervical radiculopathy. Furthermore, diagnosis of thoracic outlet syndrome requires a high index of suspicion and can be extremely complex.

Carpal tunnel syndrome is not the correct answer. Carpal tunnel syndrome is not associated with shoulder pain and sensory deficits will affect median nerve sensory distribution, rather than C6 nerve distribution (i.e. the first 3 and a half digits, with palmar sparing). Motor weakness in carpal tunnel syndrome will often manifest with weakness of thumb abduction and opposition and weakness of elbow flexion and wrist extension is not a feature. Late-stage carpal tunnel syndrome may reveal wasting of the thenar eminence.
learningPoints: Most cases of cervical radiculopathy are secondary to arthritic disease of the spine.
references: Corey DL, Comeau D. Cervical Radiculopathy. Med Clin N Am 2014; 98:791-99.
Khadilkar S, Khade S. Brachial plexopathy. Ann Indian Acad of Neurol. 2013 Jan;16(1):12–18.

Thoomes EJ, van Geest S, van der Windt DA, Falla D, Verhagen AP, Koes BW, et al. Value of physical tests in diagnosing cervical radiculopathy: a systematic review. The Spine Journal. 2018 Jan;18(1):179–89
